explain.rule: Can is a modal verb used to express: (1) ability — something you know how to do, (2) possibility — something that is possible, (3) permission — asking or giving permission to do something, (4) requests — politely asking someone to do something. Modal verbs are special: they do NOT add -s for he/she/it (she can, not she cans), they are always followed by a base verb (can go, not can to go, not can going), and they make negatives and questions WITHOUT do/does (can't, not doesn't can).
explain.why_matters: Can and can't are among the most frequently used words in English. You use them every day — to talk about what you are able to do, to ask for help, to give or ask for permission, and to talk about possibilities. Getting the structure right is essential.
explain.hebrew_note: In Hebrew, ability is expressed with יכול (yakhol) + infinitive with ל: 'אני יכול לדבר'. In English, can is simpler — it directly precedes the base verb with NO 'to': 'I can speak' (not 'I can to speak'). Also: 'יכול' changes for gender in Hebrew (יכול/יכולה) but 'can' NEVER changes — it is the same for all subjects: I can, she can, they can.
explain.tip: Can sentences follow this pattern: subject + can/can't + BASE verb (no to, no -ing, no -s). Test yourself: Can she sings? NO → Can she sing? YES. She can to drive? NO → She can drive? YES. Think of 'can' as taking the verb back to its purest, simplest form.

form.positive: Subject + can + base verb: I can swim. She can drive. They can speak French.
form.negative: Subject + can't/cannot + base verb: I can't swim. He can't drive. Cannot is more formal than can't.
form.question: Can + subject + base verb?: Can you swim? Can she drive? Can they help?
form.short_answer: Yes, I can. / No, I can't.

uses.example: It can get very cold here in winter.
Can you help me with this?

good_examples.note: Ability — something I know how to do

good_examples.sentence: She can't drive — she never learned.
good_examples.audio_text: She can't drive — she never learned.
good_examples.note: Inability — she/he/it still uses can't, no -s

good_examples.sentence: Can you help me carry this?
good_examples.audio_text: Can you help me carry this?

good_examples.sentence: You can park here for free.
good_examples.audio_text: You can park here for free.

good_examples.sentence: This city can be very busy in summer.
good_examples.audio_text: This city can be very busy in summer.

good_examples.note: Expressing disbelief — can't be = it is impossible that he is
Question about ability — no does, no -s on swim

good_examples.sentence: Dogs can hear sounds that humans can't.
good_examples.audio_text: Dogs can hear sounds that humans can't.
good_examples.note: Comparing abilities — both follow the same pattern

good_examples.sentence: I can't find my keys anywhere.
good_examples.audio_text: I can't find my keys anywhere.

bad_examples.reason: Modal verbs NEVER add -s for he/she/it. Can stays as can for all subjects.
After modal verbs, the following verb is ALWAYS base form — NO 'to'. Can swim, not can to swim.
Modal verbs don't use do/does to make questions. Just invert the modal: Can she...?
Modal verbs don't use don't/doesn't for negatives. Use can't or cannot directly.

bad_examples.wrong: Can you to open the window?
bad_examples.reason: No 'to' after can. Can + base verb, always.

exercises.question: She ___ (can / speak) four languages fluently.
exercises.hint: Modal + base verb. No -s on the modal or the main verb.
exercises.explanation: can + base verb. She can speak — can never changes, speak stays in base form.
exercises.hebrew_note: יכולה לדבר = can speak. אין -s, אין to

exercises.question: I ___ (can / not) find my glasses anywhere.
exercises.hint: Can + not = can't (or cannot)
exercises.explanation: can't or cannot — both correct. Can't is the contracted, everyday form.

exercises.question: ___ you help me move this table?
exercises.hint: Making a request — which modal?
exercises.explanation: Can you...? = polite request. The modal goes first in questions.

exercises.question: ___ I leave early today? I have an appointment.
exercises.hint: Asking permission — which word goes first?
exercises.explanation: Can I...? = asking for permission. Informal and common in everyday English.

exercises.question: He ___ (can) run 10km without stopping — he trains every day.
exercises.hint: Ability — just the modal, followed by run
exercises.explanation: He can run — ability. No -s on can for he.

exercises.question: This restaurant ___ (can / be) very noisy at the weekend.
exercises.hint: Possibility — sometimes it is noisy
exercises.explanation: can be = possibility, it sometimes happens. This city can be busy = it is sometimes busy.

exercises.question: ___ she drive, or does she take the bus?
exercises.hint: Question about ability → modal first
exercises.explanation: Can she drive? = question about ability. Invert modal and subject.

exercises.question: You ___ (can) use my laptop if you want.
exercises.hint: Giving permission to someone
exercises.explanation: You can use... = giving permission. Can + base verb.

exercises.question: I ___ (can / not) believe how expensive that hotel was.
exercises.hint: Expressing disbelief / surprise
exercises.explanation: can't believe = expressing that something is surprising or shocking.

exercises.question: Children ___ (can) enter the museum for free.
exercises.hint: Permission for a group — they are allowed to
exercises.explanation: can = they are allowed/permitted to. Children can enter free.

exercises.question: ___ you speak more slowly, please?
exercises.hint: Polite request — asking someone to do something
exercises.explanation: Can you speak more slowly? = polite request. Very common in real English.

exercises.question: I ___ (can / not) come to the meeting tomorrow — I'm travelling.
exercises.hint: Unable to attend = negative ability/possibility
exercises.explanation: can't come = not able to / not going to be able to.

exercises.question: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.answer: She can drive but she can't park.
exercises.hint: Modal verb rules: no -s, no to, no do/does
exercises.explanation: She can drive — no -s on can. She can't park — can't + base verb. Both correct.
distractors: She cans drive but she can't park.
She can to drive but she can't parking.
She can drive but she doesn't can park.

exercises.question: How do you ask permission to use someone's pen?
exercises.hint: Asking permission — modal first
exercises.explanation: Can I use your pen? = informal but polite permission request.
distractors: Do I can use your pen?
Can I to use your pen?

exercises.question: My grandfather ___ remember the names of all his grandchildren.
exercises.hint: He is not able to remember — inability
exercises.explanation: can't remember = not able to remember. No doesn't, no -s.

exercises.question: ___ he cook, or does he always eat out?
exercises.hint: Question about ability — modal inverts with subject
exercises.explanation: Can he cook? = question about ability. Does not use 'Does'.

exercises.question: This medicine ___ cause drowsiness.
exercises.hint: Possibility — it is possible that this happens
exercises.explanation: can cause = it is possible / it sometimes happens. Common in instructions and warnings.

exercises.question: You ___ sit anywhere you like.
exercises.hint: Giving permission — you are free to sit anywhere
exercises.explanation: You can sit anywhere = I give you permission / you are free to.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She cans play the piano very well.'
exercises.answer: She can play the piano very well.
exercises.hint: Do modal verbs ever add -s?
exercises.explanation: Modal verbs NEVER add -s. Can stays as can for all subjects — she can, he can.
exercises.hebrew_note: can אף פעם לא מקבל s — אפילו ל-she/he/it

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I can to speak a little French.'
exercises.answer: I can speak a little French.
exercises.hint: What comes after a modal verb?
exercises.explanation: After modal verbs: always base verb, never 'to + verb'. Can speak, not can to speak.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'Does she can drive?'
exercises.hint: How do you form questions with modal verbs?
exercises.explanation: Modal verbs make questions by inverting with the subject. No 'does'. Can she drive? NOT Does she can drive?

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'He doesn't can swim.'
exercises.hint: How do you make a negative with modal verbs?
exercises.explanation: Negatives with modals: can + not = can't/cannot. Never 'doesn't can'.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'Can you to help me?'
exercises.hint: After can — base verb or to + verb?
exercises.explanation: Can you help me? — no 'to' after can. Base verb directly.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Subject + modal + base verb
exercises.explanation: She can speak — modal + base verb, no -s on can or speak.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Can you help me carry this?
exercises.hint: Request: modal first, then subject, then base verb
exercises.explanation: Can you help me carry this? — request. Can + subject + base verb.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Negative: subject + can't + base verb
exercises.explanation: She can't come tonight — negative modal, base verb after can't.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Asking permission: modal first
exercises.explanation: Can I use your charger? — permission request.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Dogs can hear things that humans can't.
exercises.hint: Both affirmative and negative modal in same sentence
exercises.explanation: Dogs can hear (ability) + that humans can't (short negative — 'can't hear' with 'hear' omitted).

speak.sentence: Can you speak more slowly, please? I don't understand everything.
chunks: Can you speak more slowly, please?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Slowly' = SLOH-lee. 'Understand' = un-der-STAND — stress on last syllable. 'Everything' = EV-ree-thing — three syllables.

speak.sentence: I can speak English and Hebrew but I can't speak Arabic.
chunks: I can speak English and Hebrew
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Arabic' = AIR-uh-bik — stress on first syllable. 'Hebrew' = HEE-broo. 'Can't' = KANT — short 'a' sound.

speak.sentence: She can't come to the meeting tomorrow — she has a doctor's appointment.
chunks: She can't come to the meeting tomorrow —
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Tomorrow' = tuh-MOR-oh — stress on second syllable. 'Appointment' = uh-POINT-ment — stress on second syllable.

speak.sentence: Can I ask you a question about the project?
chunks: Can I ask you a question
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Question' = KWES-chun — two syllables. 'Project' = PROJ-ekt — stress on first syllable.

speak.sentence: He can run really fast but he can't swim very well.
chunks: but he can't swim very well.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Really' = REE-uh-lee — three syllables. 'Swim' — the short 'i' sound. 'Fast' = FAST.

speak.sentence: Can you tell me where the nearest bus stop is?
chunks: where the nearest bus stop is?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Nearest' = NEER-ist — two syllables, stress on first. 'Where' — the 'wh' sound, not 'v'.

speak.sentence: I can't believe how expensive everything is in this city.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Believe' = bih-LEEV — stress on second syllable. 'Expensive' = ik-SPEN-siv — stress on second.

speak.sentence: Babies can recognise their mother's voice from the day they are born.
chunks: Babies can recognise their mother's voice
from the day they are born.
